data.title: Intel's CEO Is Overwhelmed With Problems On All Fronts
data.description:
Performance Assessment

My bearish view on Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) before Q2 FY25 earnings has worked out very well as the stock has sold off hard since then:

Thesis

The problems for Intel keep piling up and getting worse:

  1. The absence of customer incentives reveals a hard truth about lower organic demand for Intel's PCs
  2. Intel continues to struggle in the data center and AI segment
  3. Intel risks falling behind technologically if they are unable to secure a customer for their 14A node
  4. There may be some valuation-related upside risk
  5. INTC vs SPX500 seems ready to breakdown after 1 year period of re-distribution
  6. A potential CEO resignation is another risk monitorable

The absence of customer incentives reveals a hard truth about lower organic demand for Intel's PCs

In my Q2 preview note for Intel, I had highlighted a strategy that the CEO Lip-Bu Tan had to salvage profitability and re-orient the company at least philosophically to earn business from customers by virtue of good products alone rather than incentives and discounts. I also perceived some signals that not everyone in the senior leadership team aligned with this strategy:

…he [Intel's CEO] leans toward lowering customer incentive payments to PC OEMs, relying instead on natural pull-ins of the customer by virtue of shipping good products. But interestingly, it looks like some of his senior leadership may not be fully aligned with this philosophy…

- Hunting Alpha's Q2 FY25 Preview Note for Intel

This strategy came into effect fully in Q2 FY25 and the result is a 5.6% drop in notebook and laptop revenues due to a lower volume of business as a result of removed incentives and discounts:

Client revenue (collectively notebook and desktop) was $6.6 billion in Q2 2025, down $390 million from Q2 2024, primarily due to lower Q2 2025 client volume resulting from incremental customer incentives offered to certain customers in Q2 2024.

- Intel's Q2 FY25 10Q

Ultimately, this had led to a small 3.3% YoY decline in revenues in the Client Computing Group [CCG] segment:

I would say this highlights a harsh reality for Intel that it has been dependent on providing customer incentives and discounts to print more revenues in the CCG business. This indicates a weaker level of true, organic demand arising from customers choosing Intel because it provides the best product for their purposes.

Now, I understand and agree with CEO's idea that you want to win business by simply shipping good products without relying on the provision of discounts and incentives to generate volume. However, perhaps they should have developed better products first before removing these incentives?

Intel continues to struggle in the data center and AI segment

The data center and AI [DCAI] business is supposed to be the big growth driver today. And indeed, that is the case for Intel's competitor Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) which is seeing >50% YoY growth in this business and rapidly gaining market share too. So that makes Intel's performance of sub-5% YoY growth all the more disappointing:

Like in the CCG business, it looks like Intel needs to resort to lower their prices in an effort to compete:

Server ASPs decreased 8% from Q2 2024, primarily due to pricing actions taken in a competitive environment.

- Intel's Q2 FY25 10Q

I am not alone in my skepticism as other analysts also quizzed the CEO with some tough questions in the latest earnings call:

I see in your 10-Q, you mentioned server ASPs are down 8% from last year because of a competitive environment. I thought that rising cores would mean greater ASP. So just if you could address that. But then kind of more medium to longer term, Lip-Bu, how impactful is competition from ARM who is claiming to take over half the server market?

- BoFA Analyst Vivek Arya in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Management's response was rather lack luster with the CEO admitting that mistakes were made and that they are listening to customers and that they have a chance to regain share with some new products. Basically more promises and hopeful commentary, but no tangible signs of improvement so far.

Intel risks falling behind technologically if they are unable to secure a customer for their 14A node

Intel has bet big on semiconductor foundry operations. But now, the future of the foundry business is at serious risk as the company is struggling to secure a major customer for its 14A node. Commentary also suggests that this risks them falling behind technologically as most of their products would be manufactured in the older generation 18A-P node until at least 2030:

We are focused on the continued development of Intel 14A, the next generation node beyond Intel 18A and Intel 18A-P, and on securing a significant external customer for such node. However, if we are unable to secure a significant external customer and meet important customer milestones for Intel 14A...we may pause or discontinue our pursuit of Intel 14A and successor nodes and various of our manufacturing expansion projects…If we were to discontinue development of Intel 14A and successor nodes, we expect that a majority of our products would continue to be manufactured in our own facilities utilizing our nodes up to Intel 18A-P through at least 2030…

We have been unsuccessful to date in securing any significant external foundry customers for any of our nodes and our prospects for securing a significant external foundry customer for Intel 14A are uncertain.

- Intel's Q2 FY25 10Q, Author's bolded highlights

In my very first article on Intel here at Seeking Alpha, I had made a case for how it is unlikely to catch up to TSMC (TSM). Now, if Intel remains stuck at the 18A technology, it risks falling even further behind TSMC's innovations in this decade:

There may be some valuation-related upside risk

INTC is trading at a 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA of 8.39x, which implies a 10% premium to the long term median of 7.63x. Given the broad-based challenges faced, I think this premium is unwarranted for INTC.

But relative to its comps' median of 20.7x, INTC is trading at a 59% discount. So I also recognize that there may be some valuation-related upside risk here.

INTC vs SPX500 seems ready to breakdown after 1 year period of re-distribution

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of INTC vs SPX500

In the context of a long and powerful downtrend, INTC stock relative to the SPX500 has been in a bi re-distribution range. And more recently, a strong seller push has initiated a potential breakdown to the downside. Hence, I expect continued underperformance to follow.

A potential CEO resignation is another risk monitorable

Intel's CEO Lip-Bu Tan clearly has a lot on his plate to turn Intel around. But recently, there are new issues that are bound to take away his limited and valuable bandwidth. Lip-Bu Tan is under scrutiny for alleged ties to China from a US lawmaker Senator Cotton.

President Trump has made this issue take center-stage with the following bold post on Truth Social, outrightly calling for the resignation of Intel's CEO:

The CEO of INTEL is highly CONFLICTED and must resign, immediately. There is no other solution to this problem. Thank you for your attention to this problem!

- Trump's post on Truth Social

I suspect that an unsaid reason for Trump's motivations to oust Intel's CEO may be his dismay at Intel potentially culling its foundry business. This is because such a move would hinder the US' plans to have its own companies run major semiconductor foundries, diversifying away from Taiwan's TSMC (TSM) and Korea's Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF).

In any case, I am watching the Polymarket's prediction market odds of Intel's CEO resigning by the end of this month as a key risk monitorable:

Currently, the odds are at 15%, which I think is not minute at all. I expect such an event to lead to further turmoil in INTC stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

Intel is facing revenue challenges in all its business segments. In the CCG segment, the removal of customer incentive discounts has exposed lower organic demand for Intel's PCs. In the DCAI segment, it is falling far behind its competitors as it shows pathetic sub-5% YoY growth in the hottest growth trend in the semiconductor space today. And the future of the foundry business that it has banked on is now at risk as it is struggling to secure a major external customer for its 14A node. This in turn is increasing the risk of it falling even further behind TSMC and even Samsung Electronics.

But that's not where the barrage of challenges end; there is fresh political pressure from President Trump calling for the new CEO to step down, with the world's largest prediction market putting the odds of this event occurring by the end of this month at a worrying 15%. I think these risks are not yet reflected in the stock price, which seems to be on the cusp of a breakdown vs the S&P500. The only major solace is that INTC's valuations are at a decent ~60% discount vs peers. But I would say this is hardly sufficient to change my view:

Rating: 'Underperform/Sell'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals: Feeling The Effects Of The Aethon Time Out (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
Thesis

Black Stone Minerals (NYSE:BSM) reported second quarter earnings that generated adequate distribution coverage to the now "right-sized" distribution level. This distribution level allows the firm to allocate capital to building the company's inventory levels while it manages declining production.

This decline in production can be traced to the Aethon "time-out" that was exercised in early 2024. The timeout has interrupted the replacement cadence of BSM's production volumes. As a result, most of the legacy Aethon wells have likely reached maturity with quickly declining production.

The company is diligently working to develop a diverse contract mix on its acreage and expects production to pick up in 2026. As a result of this new production forecast, I anticipate distribution coverage will decline to 1.05-1.1x in late 2025 but stands to rebound significantly in 2026.

I have downgraded the stock to HOLD on the belief that investors are currently too bullish and neglecting near term challenges that must be tackled before reaching a more promising 2026.

Highlights From The Q2 Earnings Report

In June, I covered BSM in the article titled "Black Stone Minerals: Bringing Oil Back" outlining the anticipation of a strong rebound in 2026 oil production. In that same article I acknowledged the fact that the BSM's declining production may present a challenge to sustain the quarterly distribution of $0.38/unit.

As it turns out, management did cut the distribution to $0.30/unit. This has allowed the company to reinvest $30 million in excess cash toward purchasing new mineral rights. When this was announced in mid-July, the move appeared to be a prudent course of action as it would help address the company's low inventory concerns.

However, fast forward a few weeks and management has also indicated that 2025 production is declining more abruptly that I had previously anticipated. The company announced that full year production guidance had been lowered to 33-35 MBOE/d, a rather significant drop (14%) from the previous guidance of 38-41 MBOE/d.

As shown below, natural gas production fell nearly 9% in the quarter. This is in addition to commodity price declines in both natural gas and crude oil. The combined impact more than offset the strong rebound in crude oil production that BSM generated in the quarter.

At the new distribution level, the current production was able to generate a distribution coverage of 1.18x. This gives the company significantly more margin than BSM has displayed in recent history. However, in a declining production scenario this isn't a very practical forward-looking indicator.

To address this, I modeled BSM's production at 33 MBOE/d in two different ways. In the first method, 100% of the production decline was from the company's natural gas production. The second method assumed that half of the production decline was associated with oil production which carries a significantly higher monetary value. Under these conditions, I expect the current distribution coverage to drop to 1.05-1.10x in the second half of 2025.

Slightly further out, the company expects production to recover modestly to 36-40 MBOE in 2026. This stems from an additional 3-5 MBOE of new production that is expected to come online across the portfolio. These new volumes should have a meaningful oil cut as the 34 well row project in Culberson County is expected to be turned to sales starting in Q4.

For forward modeling, I have assumed a forward strip price of $3.50/MCF and $65/barrel of oil. Under these conditions, I expect single digit revenue growth in 2026 compared to the revenue reported in Q2.

2026 revenue should be further strengthened by an enhanced hedging profile. Over 40% of 2026 natural gas production is currently hedged at $3.67/MMBtu, giving the company a modest floor of support. Under these conditions, I expect 2026 distribution coverage to range between 1.25-1.30x.

What's Causing The Steep Production Decline?

US shale plays are known for their extremely high IP rates. The downside is that these rates don't last long with production declining as early as 6 months after a well first comes online. This varies from basin to basin but generally after a year of production, the well is producing less than 1/3 of peak production rates.

This goes hand in hand with the Aethon "timeout" that occurred in early 2024. At the time, natural gas prices had dipped to sub-$2/mcf levels. That price was clearly unsustainable for Haynesville natural gas wells and Aethon put a halt to future drill activity. To do so, the company exercised its timeout provision in its drilling contract with BSM.

Over the following year, BSM was fortunate to realize some fresh activity on its acreage. The bulk of this activity was focused on capturing peak winter prices by completing a few of the DUCs that were in process at the time the timeout was declared. The replacement volumes showed themselves with a brief spike in volumes in Q1, but even these wells are in their first stages of decline.

Fortunately, the timeout provision has since been rescinded but bringing production back online takes time and is ultimately influenced by the commodity price. As of this writing, we are in the shoulder season for natural gas and prices have dipped below $3/MCF. This makes leaning into more production in a high-cost basin like the Haynesville, a difficult sell.

Giving The Portfolio A Nudge In The Right Direction

BSM is attempting to expedite the recovery by diversifying its development agreements to multiple operators. In the previous quarter, BSM signed Revenant Energy to a similar operator structure to that which Aethon currently operates under.

Under the terms of this agreement, Revenant is expected to increase its activity annually while also drilling a handful of test wells to help BSM prove its undeveloped acreage. This agreement will operate simultaneous to the 18-well per year agreement BSM has with Aethon.

BSM's management also indicated in the second quarter conference call that it was in early discussions with a third operator to develop a separate 180,000 gross acres track in the Shelby Trough. Part of this development package was reclaimed from Aethon and early commentary from management suggest that this effort is aimed at developing a connection between the Shelby Trough and recent results in the Western Haynesville.

The Western Haynesville is being pioneered by Comstock Resources (CRK). The company has reported significant IP rates in the region, with some wells peaking out at over 40 MMCF/d. If BSM is successful in attracting investment from yet another private producer, it becomes easier to see how BSM holds the potential to snow ball production in the later part of this decade.

Valuation

Given the steady state decline in production over the last 18 months, it should not be too surprising to see the company's valuation deteriorate. The company now trades at roughly 10x EBITDA. However with just $71 million in total debt, and very little capital required to run a royalty business model, most of this cash gets delivered to the shareholders. That is how the company can deliver a 9.5% yield while still trading at 10x EBITDA.

However on a forward basis the earnings profile is much more attractive. Forward estimates have the company trading at less than 8x earnings. This parallels earnings estimates which forecast 2026 EPS at $1.49/share, up over 10% from 2025 estimates.

Both of these indicators point toward reasonable future value, but I am a bit surprised how quickly the stock recovered following the announced distribution cut. In roughly a month, the stock recovered from its more than 10% drop. As it stands today, the distribution is more or less in line with the 5-year average despite the near term challenges in 2025 and early 2026.

This makes me leary that investors are being too bullish and may be too comfortable with the 1.18x distribution coverage headline. There remains plenty of work to be done to successfully bridge the gap between now and mid-2026 when volumes should be on an upswing. For this reason I have downgraded the stock to a HOLD but would not be opposed to improving this rating to a BUY should the stock decline into the low $12 range.

Risks

Whether we like it or not, natural gas demand is heavily dominated by winter weather. Strong production combined with a seasonally warm winter allowed for record inventories in the spring of 2024. The most recent winter resulted in significantly more demand, thereby normalizing inventories.

The anticipation of strong winter prices led to higher completions activity over the winter months. This has allowed inventories to recover quickly, despite starting the spring nearly 800 BCF lower than the previous year. If winter 2026 turns out to be more mild than normal, we could again see natural gas in the $2-$3 range.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. BSM's production is steadily declining and lowered 2025 production guidance by 14%.

2. The investment community has more or less paid no attention to this fact with the stock now nearly fully recovered from its announced distribution cut in mid-July.

3. 2026 continues to hold the promise of a recovery in both oil and natural gas volumes. However, production is falling faster than replacement volumes can be developed. This creates a near term hurdle the company must clear.

4. The current set up makes me believe investors are being overly optimistic. Maybe they saw 1.18x distribution coverage and carried on with their day, or maybe I'm just being too pessimistic. Maybe the situation is a little of both.

5. Even after accounting for the previously mentioned production declines, I still forecast at least 1.05x distribution coverage in year end 2025. The margins are thin but BSM has a solid balance sheet and reasonable enough 2026 prospects that another distribution cut should not be necessary at current market prices.

data.title: NCR Atleos Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by NCR Atleos Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Geron: I Think We've Seen This Movie Before
data.description:
Geron overview

Geron Corporation (NASDAQ:GERN) is down about 70% in the past 12 months, with a mixed start to the launch of Rytelo (imetelstat) not helping matters. I rated GERN a buy in March, noting periods of flatness in sales can happen during the launch of a drug, but I doubted that Rytelo had already reached its peak market share just a few quarters into its launch. This article looks at some of the signs of growth for GERN's Rytelo launch following this week's Q2'25 earnings, and updates to the company's trials.

GERN now, reminds me of AUPH in early 2024

With Q2'25 earnings, GERN reported net product revenue of $49M for the quarter, up from $39.4M in Q1'25. Unfortunately these numbers only represent a modest increase from Q4'24, so there isn't a really clear trend of growth by this metric. Importantly, the underlying demand growth that GERN notes for Q2'25 vs Q1'25 of 17% means that Q2'25 performance can be attributed in large part to demand, not simply refilling the channel or otherwise. Indeed with Q1'25 earnings, GERN noted inventory drawdown at the distributor as a factor in falling net product revenue vs Q4'24, but suggested underlying demand growth of 1% in Q1'25 vs Q4'24.

GERN's current situation reminds me of the situation Aurinia Pharmaceuticals (AUPH) found itself in during early-to-mid 2024. At that time AUPH's stock was languishing following three quarters of fairly flat sales (Q2'23, Q3'24 and Q4'23), with some concerns that their sole marketed drug (Lupkynis) might have reached a plateau. Indeed with AUPH it was slightly more plausible that Lupkynis had reached an early plateau with minimal sales growth ahead since the drug had been on the market for three years. In that case however, Q1'24 earnings confirmed a breakout from three quarters of fairly flat sales, with the stock now up over 100% since that time.

With GERN it is even less plausible that sales have reached an early plateau than in the AUPH situation, given how early we are into the launch of Rytelo and GERN refining its marketing strategy. On the other hand, GERN's recent earnings sales numbers don't quite reflect a breakout from the flat period of sales, forcing us to rely on GERN's statements about underlying demand growth. Further, bulls might argue that we could see a clearer confirmation of Rytelo performance in the future, given some of the comments the company is making.

Firstly, in Q2'25 and 2025 in general, more sites have used Rytelo, meaning new physicians are making Rytelo part of their armamentarium. Further, the drug isn't just seeing use in third-line and beyond (3L+) lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (LR-MDS).

... At the end of the second quarter, there were approximately 1,000 sites of care that have utilized RYTELO launched to date. This is an increase of approximately 400 new sites since the beginning of the year.

Of the accounts that previously ordered, approximately 2/3 have reordered in the second quarter. The rolling 3-month claims data as of May 2025 estimates that approximately 30% of RYTELO new patient starts were in first and second lines...

James Ziegler, Executive VP and CCO of GERN, Q2'25 earnings call.

Note that GERN noted with Q1'25 earnings that it expected to see an increase in usage in earlier lines of therapy, but was only seeing 25% of its use there at that time.

The rolling 3-month market claims data as of February 2025 estimates that approximately 25% of RYTELO new patient starts were in the 1st and 2nd lines. We expect to see an increase in second line use as HCPs gain more experience with RYTELO and become more comfortable prescribing it in earlier settings.

Jim Ziegler, CCO of GERN, Q1'25 earnings call, emphasis mine.

GERN might have made some headway then, pushing use of Rytelo in earlier lines of therapy, and this will be an important metric to track each quarter provided GERN reports these numbers (an absence of such report might suggest there hasn't been progress, so that alone is worth monitoring).

The company completing training of the extra commercial staff it announced earlier in 2025 can also help increase GERN's chances of showing Rytelo growth.

By expanding our commercial sales force and customer-facing roles by over 20% and doubling the size of our medical affairs organization, we believe that we're on track to achieve the KOL support warranted by RYTELO's unique mechanism of action and differentiated therapeutic profile. Our new hires have completed training and are now deployed to educate and inform key accounts and HCPs. We expect to begin seeing their impact by year-end.

Dawn Carter Bir, Interim President & CEO of GERN, Q2'25 earnings call.

It sounds like the benefit of these personnel increases might not manifest in Q3'25 earnings however, but instead bodes well for driving sales growth beyond that.

Additional Financials

For Q2'25, GERN reported; R&D expense of $21.8M, SG&A expense of $38.6M and cost of goods sold of $1.2M. Net loss was $16.4M for the quarter. GERN maintained its guidance for FY2025 total operating expenses of $270M-$285M.

GERN finished Q2'25 with $432.6M in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and marketable securities. The company drew $125M via its Pharmakon Loan Agreement at the end of 2024, although that loan doesn't mature until 2029.

Further, GERN's Royalty Pharma Agreement, which brought in $125M upfront in November 2024, means GERN has to pay a fraction of US net sales to Royalty Pharma, although this could cease once GERN has made payments equal to 1.65 times the "Purchase Price" of $125M ($206.25) provided GERN gets there by June 30, 2031.

... Royalty Pharma obtained the right, or the Revenue Participation Right, to receive certain amounts calculated as a percentage of future U.S. net sales of RYTELO for each calendar quarter, or Royalty Payments, during the term contemplated by the Royalty Pharma Agreement. Specifically, the revenue participation rate commences at 7.75% for annual U.S. net sales of up to and equal to $500.0 million declining to 1.0% for annual U.S. net sales exceeding $1.0 billion until the date when the aggregate Royalty Payments equal or exceed 1.65 times the Purchase Price, if this occurs by June 30, 2031 or the date when the aggregate Royalty Payments equal or exceed 2.0 times the Purchase Price.

Comments from GERN's 10-Q, filed August 2025.

GERN accounts for the agreement as a financing liability and currently notes a carrying value for that liability of $127.97M as of June 30, 2025. In any case, GERN believes its cash plus revenues from Rytelo will fund operations into the future. Aided by revenue growth, GERN's net loss has narrowed since Q3'24 when the company reported sales from its first full quarter of Rytelo sales.

I believe GERN could be just a few quarters away from reporting net incomes. Regarding net cash used in operating activities, GERN noted net loss of $19.8M in Q1'25, but net cash used in operating activities of $48.0M, part of that difference came from a $17.5M change in inventory.

Kalpit Patel - B. Riley Securities

Okay, and then one financial question, you reported 19.8 million net loss for the quarter and had about 45 million in cash burn. Maybe walk us through what drove that incremental 25 million in outflow; was this, any capital changes or inventory build or something else?

Michelle Robertson - Chief Financial Officer

Well Q1 cash burn is always higher due to payouts of bonuses and some early investment, yes, on the inventory side...

GERN has continued to build its inventory, now noting $69.1M on the balance sheet at the end of Q2'25, up from $56.2M at the end of Q1'25. GERN now reports net cash used in operating activities of $75.4M in the first six months of 2025, suggesting net cash used of $27.4M in Q2'25, with a large part of the gap between that and the net loss of $16.4M being explained by further inventory increases. In any case, if GERN stopped increasing its inventory at the current rate, cash flow positivity might not follow too far behind reporting a net income, and again I believe that might be just a few quarters away, especially if GERN can maintain growth over 10% from quarter-to-quarter.

Risks

There are several risks to a long in GERN, a few of which I'll note here.

Firstly, if Q3'25 earnings demonstrate growth too modest to convince the market that GERN's sales push is having a benefit, then the stock could fall. If there is no growth, or even a drop in sales, then the fall in the stock could be even greater.

Secondly, GERN's launch in Europe could face delays, which would impact analyst estimates for revenues adversely. Even if GERN launches on time, slow initial progress or updates, even on the individual countries in which the launch is underway, might give the impression the drug isn't well received in Europe.

Lastly, the timelines for a readout from GERN's clinical studies, including IMpactMF have seen revisions previously. With IMpactMF 95% enrolled, the possibility of slippage would be more dependent on a lower than expected rate of events, rather than delays in enrolling the trial, but nonetheless the possibility exists of having to wait beyond H2'26. Confirmation of slippage in the timeline could cause some to sell the stock and come back later or not at all.

Conclusions and GERN stock rating

I think GERN is likely to show more resoundingly in coming quarters, if not with Q3'25 earnings, that there is a lot more to come in terms of revenues from Rytelo. Q2'25 earnings haven't quite done that, although the stock has responded positively which might suggest some are reading into management's comments (which indeed sound more positive than the comments warning about flatness of sales that we heard in early 2025). I can see a situation where a few quarters demonstrating that revenues from Rytelo are indeed growing could dovetail nicely with a run up into an interim readout from IMpactMF (expected H2'26) which might begin in H1'26.

In the short-term, the obvious catalyst would simply be Q3'25 earnings, and so perhaps the more compelling strategy is a buy and hold throughout the next few quarters, reconsidering the position with each update on Rytelo sales. It would also be prudent to monitor progress on the EU launch (a launch in expected timelines) and any slippage or confirmation of the timeline for the IMpactMF interim analysis (the interim is based on a given number events, so can move forward or back). I'm willing to rate GERN a buy here, although my timeline for success isn't short term, the risk that Q3'25 doesn't quite deliver enough growth to impress the market must be considered as a near-term risk.

data.title: Federal Realty Trust: The Dividend King Of Real Estate
data.description:
Real estate is one of the most cyclical businesses on the planet. Among the different property types, everything from social changes to macroeconomic reshaping can affect the demand curve of just about everything. Over the years, asset classes have ebbed and flowed in a variety of ways, some of which are to be expected while others have a tendency to catch investors by surprise.

Recently, the commercial real estate market has been defined by oversupply among concentrated asset classes like life science, industrial, and more recently, multifamily. What can once act as a demand driver can quickly turn on investors quickly causing demand to evaporate. In the case of multifamily specifically, the demographic explosion of the sunbelt has come to a screeching halt as hot cities such as Atlanta begin to report problematic demographic trends, causing turmoil for multifamily REITs.

Long before the multifamily market began to show signs of weakness, there was another problem child in the commercial real estate industry. Shopping centers and malls were the redheaded stepchildren of real estate as an online retailer called the Amazon (AMZN) began to smash the demand for brick and mortar retail. The ability for shoppers to get their goods at home within hours if not days became an existential crisis for shopping centers whose value proposition lied heavily upon their strategic positioning close to wealthy households. In an era where shoppers no longer needed to leave their living room to receive goods at an accelerated pace, the demand for high quality, expensive shopping centers fundamentally came into question.

The issues around shopping centers were further exacerbated by the pandemic which saw other retailers which were once dependent upon their brick and mortar footprints begin to shift towards a more digital footprint to reach their clientele. This meant further chaos as some of the core tenants of shopping centers began to shift away from their reliance on retail space. Finally, after nearly ten years in the penalty box, shopping centers have begun to reemerge as an attractive asset class based on fundamentals including property locations and rising demand. The precarious positioning of the past decade has provided insight as to who are some of the best and brightest operators inside of the industry.

The purpose of today's article is to highlight a company who is just that, one of the best and brightest in the shopping center industry. Federal Realty Investment Trust (NYSE:FRT) currently holds the record for the longest streak of annual dividend increases of any publicly traded REIT, raising the dividend for 57 years consecutively.

With that, let’s initiate coverage on the only Dividend King in real estate.

REIT Overview

FRT is one of the largest dedicated owner operators of shopping center real estate in the United States. The company owns more than 100 properties covering around 27 million square feet of retail space and over 3,000 units of multifamily. In total, the footprint of FRT's portfolio covers more than 2,100 acres. As you would expect with any shopping center owner, tenancy is diverse with more than 3,500 commercial tenants.

Although FRT is a shopping center REIT, the properties are more oriented towards mixed-use when viewed from a high level. Based on guidance from management, the portfolio is split by income to 79% retail, 12% residential, and 10% towards mixed-use office. The company provides a breakdown of format types within their portfolio. mixed-use centers account for the largest allocation at 36%, followed by grocery anchored community centers and neighborhood centers at 30% and 20%, respectively, the main differentiator being size. The remaining 14% is invested in power centers and other ancillary retail formats.

From a market standpoint, FRT focuses heavily on infill suburbs of nine major U.S. markets. FRT invests inside the MSAs of Washington DC, Boston, New York, Philadelphia, Chicago, Miami, and Phoenix. The company also invests in shopping centers located throughout the state of California, capitalizing on the state's high incomes and growing population centers. Demographics, specifically population and income, are important points of emphasis for any retail operator, but especially FRT. Investing in assets located close to affluent areas provides an ample stream of discretionary income to be spent at the many tenants leasing space from FRT. According to FRT, the average household income within 3 miles of one of their properties is $161,000.

I often differentiate categories of real estate operators. Typically, two of the biggest points of differentiation that I draw are between holders and operators. Holders are those who typically own real estate passively, such as net lease REITs that buy long leased assets. On the other hand, there are operators who treat real estate more like a business looking at ways to generate value on a continuous and ongoing basis, rather than depending on long term, predetermined cash flow. Perhaps there is no better example of an operating business than shopping centers.

From an operational standpoint, shopping centers have struggled over the past 10 years, facing weak demand, rising costs, and questionable consumer health. In the face of a difficult time, operational performance from FRT has begun to improve. Currently, the portfolio is about 94% occupied, and the leased rate is around 96%, highlighting growing demand for retail space.

While FRT is a long term investor, the company is constantly identifying and executing on value add opportunities at existing and new properties. Right now, the company is working on around a billion dollars in redevelopment and expansion projects with key projects like Santana West in San Jose, CA and 915 Meeting St. in Bethesda, MD. Activity is an important piece of the business as FRT tries to continually create value for investors in their existing portfolio of properties.

FRT maintains a relatively conservative balance sheet. Net debt to EBITDA is now 5.4x, down from 5.7x in the prior quarter, and almost 90% of the company's debt is fixed rate. The company has over a billion dollars in liquidity including a $1.25 billion revolving credit facility. Like many real estate companies, FRT has an at-the-market equity program which can further provide capital for acquisitions, redevelopments, or expansions. The company's balance sheet has investment grade ratings from major ratings agencies.

The company remains active with large acquisitions in their sights and management hinting towards a desire to continue expanding the business creatively. In the prior quarter, management discussed the sale of two assets or $143 million with an additional $400 million on the market.

Performance & Dividend

Let’s start by acknowledging that FRT has one of the most impressive dividends in real estate, boasting 57 consecutive years of increases.

Just recently, FRT raised their quarterly dividend by 3% to $1.13 per share, marking their 58th consecutive annual increase to the dividend. Despite swimming upstream against shopping center headwinds, FRT has still grown the dividend by around 2.5% annually for the past ten years. Increases have roughly aligned with inflation over the past 30 years as the company has raised the dividend by around 2.5% annually since 1990.

Investors have become increasingly weary of FRT over the past ten years. Shares peaked in 2016 when shopping centers were still benefiting from tailwinds and had yet to encounter the beast of AMZN. Once the online behemoth began gobbling up retailers and forcing a wave of bankruptcies, shopping center REITs like FRT began to feel the burn. The company’s stock price tells a very different story before and after this turning point nearly a decade ago. Share price has declined somewhat steadily since 2016 peaks.

As shopping centers sat in the doldrums, there appeared to be few drivers for FRT to gather optimism or turn around the ship.

It turns out cyclicality might have been the saving grace of shopping centers. The pain facing shopping centers became obvious around ten years ago. This forced developers to pivot and there has been significantly less shopping center development over the past ten years compared to prior periods. The basic laws of supply and demand dictate that dwindling supply combined with rising demand is a positive driver for existing supply. Such is becoming the case for shopping centers. Following ten dry years, there appears to be a degree of undersupply in the market as investors begin paying attention to these large, attractively located assets.

From this perspective, FRT could be ready for a turnaround. The company’s shares are currently trading at a dividend yield of nearly 5% with a conservative payout ratio and impressive track record of navigating difficulties. This income stream alone highlights an attractive return profile should a degree of enthusiasm return.

Let’s pivot to the outlook for shopping centers.

Outlook

More recently, these positive tailwinds have been hampered by issues including labor inflation and consumer uncertainty. Year to date, tariffs and other macroeconomic issues have begun to impact tenant demand. According to Cushman & Wakefield, macro issues are affecting consumer spending negatively impacted the demand for space across the United States.

Retailers taking a wait-and-see approach. Abrupt shifts in tariff and trade policy are creating uncertainty for retailers and contributing to a more cautious leasing environment. Real estate costs were already a concern due to rising market rents and store fit-out costs, and many retailers are looking to reduce costs or pause leasing decisions until more clarity emerges. Additionally, store closures have been outpacing openings since the second half of last year.

Looking forward, some of these uncertainties need to quell to see the shopping center market turnaround accelerate. Right now, the market in undergoing what Cushman & Wakefield calls a recalibration as shopping centers continue to digest the changes brought on by rising interest rates, inflation, and a continued shift in consumer behavior. Following several years of positive net absorption, absorption has turned negative in 2025 with vacancy rising modestly and rent growth appearing sluggish, but still positive.

The uncertainty in the macro picture has led to another decline in new development starts. According to Colliers, contributing factors such as elevated construction costs continue to challenge builders and drive activity lower.

Elevated construction costs, now 30%–40% above pre-pandemic levels, are limiting new retail developments. In 2025, less than 20 million square feet of retail space will be delivered, well below historical averages. Despite strong demand, these cost pressures challenge developers to justify new investments, and instead push retailers to optimize and innovate within existing spaces.

While the shopping center industry has turned a new leaf over the past several years, emerging uncertainties continue to impact FRT’s outlook. A key piece of the puzzle for FRT will be leveraging high quality locations to drive performance through downcycles.

Conclusion

Right now, FRT needs a catalyst to drive change in the retail market around the United States. From one perspective, a significant amount of this pain has been priced in for FRT shares. Right now, shares are priced with an attractive yield because long term growth looks questionable. Despite continuing to raise the dividend like clockwork, shares have continued to experience protracted declines as the shopping center market slowly recovers from a prolonged period of pain. With such continued uncertainty around tariffs, labor dynamics, and consumer spending, it becomes difficult to garner enthusiasm around FRT in the short term.

Long term investors should find a degree of value in the nearly 5% yield being paid by common shares and the company's continued efforts to improve their overall leverage metrics and operational performance. The company has clearly been able to buck the trend of soft leasing, reaching their highest leased rate this past quarter. FRT earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Green Thumb Industries: Higher Revenues For Q2 But Gives Weak Q3 Outlook
data.description:
Green Thumb Industries Inc. (OTCQX:GTBIF) reported an increase in revenues for Q2 2025. The increase is due to higher sales within the company’s Consumer Packaged Goods segment. The company reported a net loss due to loss from the sale of assets. Green Thumb Industries expects minimal or nominal revenue growth for Q3 2025.

The stock price has decreased over 36% over the last 12 months, but it increased 26% over the last month. It has increased around 27% since I covered the company last May on Seeking Alpha. I continue my rating of a Hold for the company. Although there are rumors of cannabis reform at the federal level, nothing has been officially announced.

Operations and Synergies

Green Thumb Industries is a U.S. cannabis MSO (Multi-State Operator), which grows, manufactures, wholesales, and retails cannabis products. The company has 105 cannabis retail stores under the banner Rise. It operates 20 manufacturing and grow facilities. The company conducts business in 14 states: California, Connecticut, Florida, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Nevada, New Jersey, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, and Virginia. Minnesota makes up its newest cannabis recreational market and the company expects revenue from retail sales in Q3 or Q4, depending.

The company has seven cannabis brands: &Shine, Beboe, Dogwalkers, Doctor Solomon’s, Good Green, incredibles, and RHYTHM. It manufactures and sells dried flower, pre-rolls, concentrates, vapes, ingestibles, edibles, tinctures, and topicals. Besides retail, the company has robust wholesale and distribution operations, which it labels Consumer Packaged Goods (CPG). Green Thumb Industries also manufactures, distributes, and sells hemp derived THC drinks and products.

Green Thumb Industries is an investor with Agrify (AGFY). Agrify used to innovate grow equipment and grow systems, but it now manufacturers and sells cannabis products. Agrify is making hemp-derived THC drinks called Senorita THC Margaritas. Green Thumbs Industries is helping the company distribute their drinks. The company is also licensing its THC beverage brand, Rythm, to Agrify and has sold Agrify some of its assets.

Green Thumb Industries plans to increase its Consumer Packaged Goods operations in several states because it sees its largest revenue growth with this segment.

Green Thumb Industries Q2 2025 Results

Green Thumb Industries reported Q2-2025 revenue of $293.3 million, a 4.68% increase YoY and a 4.93% increase QoQ. Gross profit was $146.3 million, a 2.83% decrease YoY and a 2% increase QoQ. Gross margin was 49.88% compared to 53.73% YoY and 51.27% QoQ. The decrease in profit margin was due to price compression at the retail level.

The company attributed its revenue increase to higher sales in its Consumer Packaged Goods segment. CPG increased 17% YoY from higher sales in New York and Ohio. Revenue from retail sales slightly increased. Same-store sales YoY decreased 4%.

Cash flow from operations was $56 million. The company reported a net loss of $0.6 million compared to a net income of $20.7 YoY and a net income of $8.3 million QoQ. The net loss was caused by a $12 million loss on an asset sale, otherwise the company would have reported a net income of $11 million. Cash on hand was $177 million and working capital was $226 million. Current debt outstanding is $250 million.

The company expects flat revenue growth for Q3 2025. If Minnesota recreational sales begin during the quarter, then there may be more growth. Market consensus for the company’s Q3 earnings is $288.17 million, a decrease YoY and QoQ. The company reports in November.

Revenues have been increasing quarter to quarter. Q3 is expected to decrease. Gross profit has decreased and may continue to do so because of price compressions in the market. Operating expenses remain high because of CapEx and opening new stores. Net loss should return to net income because it was caused by asset sales. The company has not reported receivables or payables yet. It is unclear if it reported free cash flow for the quarter.

The company remains undervalued. Its stock price trades below its book value and NAV per share value. Its forward multipliers, like NTM total EV / Revenues, also imply undervaluation. Assets outweigh liabilities and debt remains in proportion to revenues. The stock price has gained over the last month likely around rumors of federal cannabis reform. It is unlikely that the price will continue to rise because of weak investor sentiment.

The MSO stock prices have gained a little over the last month. Earnings releases and rumors of federal reform are fueling upward momentum. The stock prices are still down over the last year. It is not clear when the trend will change or when federal reform will occur.

Curaleaf has the largest operations and revenues, including international sales, and leads the pack in terms of performance. Trulieve comes in second place and has the largest number of retail locations. Green Thumb Industries sits in third place compared to its peers. It has the potential to outperform them in terms of revenues, net income, and cash flow.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Green Thumb Industries is at low risk of poor performance. The company has excellent forward momentum and plenty of resources to continue expansion. Cannabis reform at the federal level will boost the company’s operations.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a low to moderate risk. Investor sentiment remains weak on the cannabis sector due to the lack of reform. Prices may drop again before higher levels are reached. Green Thumb Industries has excellent performance but it is not necessarily reflected in the stock price.

Conclusion

Green Thumb Industries reported strong financial results for Q2 2025. The company saw revenues increase YoY and QoQ. Consumer Packaged Goods is the company’s fastest growing segment. The company expects weaker performance for Q3. I continue my rating of a Hold for the company.

data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: 2 BDCs With Unsustainable Yields
data.description:
Right before we entered the Q2 earnings season, I issued a bit more strategic piece on the BDC segment, titled: BDC Investors: Prepare For An Imminent Market Rotation.

The essence of the article was about the key sector-wide drivers, which, in my opinion, will cause a significant pain for the majority of BDC players. While you can see more details in the article itself, these are the three concerns I have:

  1. BDCs are not cheap (i.e., on average priced very close to NAV with the higher quality ones having almost record valuations).
  2. Spread tightening is still a real problem negatively affecting almost all BDCs on the ground.
  3. The current sector average dividend coverage indicates that there is almost no margin of safety left.

What these points do not include is the likely scenario of having lower base rates quite soon. As I have articulated many times before, lower interest rates will translate to incremental headwinds for BDCs (e.g., take a look at Q4, 2024 BDC NII per share dynamics, which reflected the effects from the previous cuts).

Given this, my recommendation was to avoid speculative names, focus on high quality and/or BDCs with notable discounts that have relatively robust portfolios and significant margin of safety on the dividend coverage front. On top of this, my suggestion was to take a step back and really make sure that the BDCs do not consume too aggressive share of your total portfolio exposure.

Now we have multiple BDCs with already published Q2 earnings reports.

I have to say that the thesis has been playing out. So, in this article I would like to elaborate on two BDCs, where I see a heightened dividend cut risk. On both of these BDCs I had issued dividend cut alert articles before the Q2 earnings season.

#1 - OCSL, 11.9%

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (NASDAQ:OCSL) is a traditional BDC, which focuses predominantly on the senior secured first lien segment, where the companies have already private equity sponsors sitting in their capital structures. Up until 2024, OCSL was considered a blue-chip BDC with sustainable NAV and dividends.

Yet, the things started to change, when the portfolio write-downs became a systematic and material issue. The sector-wide headwinds did not come in handy either, to say the least.

As I predicted in 2024, OCSL announced a dividend cut earlier this year, trimming down its quarterly base distribution rate by 27%. Plus, the BDC relaxed the management fee, injected fresh equity and introduced a temporary incentive fee waiver.

Considering these changed and the fact that even after the cut the BDC was trading at a double digit discount, the sentiment toward OCSL clearly improved.

However, my view was that one dividend cut will not be enough and that we will be likely talking about another yield revision in either already 2025 or 2026.

Now, if we look at the Q2, 2025 earning report, I think that it is rather obvious that the current dividend yield is far from being sustainable.

Here is the thing.

The adjusted NII per share came in at $0.37, which is a steep decline compared to what was generated in the prior quarter - a result of $0.45 per share. The drop was driven by continued pressures on the spreads, additional write-downs, smaller asset size (tightly correlated to write-downs) and lower fees from repayment activity, which in the previous quarter was at abnormal levels.

While all of these variables are indeed restrictive for solid earnings generation, my main concern is the spread aspect, where we can see that based on the most recent new loan investment transactions, there is approximately 100 basis points of a delta between the total portfolio yield and what OCSL can access now in the market.

100 basis points is a material difference, especially considering the current dividend coverage, which indicates insufficient NII per share for fully matching the quarterly base distribution cash requirement (i.e., coverage of ~0.92x).

Theoretically, OCSL has an instrument available that is called: additional debt. Currently, the debt to equity is 0.99x, which is below the typical BDC sweet spot range of 1.1x to 1.25x. By taking more debt to grow the portfolio, the NII per share could indeed be brought above the base dividend cash flow requirement.

However, I would not buy this argument because of the following reasons:

  • Likely lower interest rates, which (if cuts really take place) will inevitably reduce the NII per share.
  • Further pressures to come from a continued spread compression.
  • Not that distant probability of some non-accruals popping up again.

In a nutshell, I would be very surprised if OCSL does not cut its dividend in the Q4, 2024 to Q2, 2026 period.

#2 - FSK, 13.8%

FS KKR Capital (NYSE:FSK) is structurally a similar BDC to OCSL with a clear focus on the larger, more established businesses and their first-lien senior secured loans. What these BDCs have also in common is the problem with the dividend sustainability. The difference, however, is that FSK has not yet made its first dividend cut (at least if we speak about the past couple of years), while OCSL has already executed it earlier this year.

Just as for OCSL, I had issued a specific piece on FSK before the Q2, 2025 earnings came out. The title of that article conveys the key message nicely - FS KKR: The Dividend Cut Risk Is High, I'm Still Avoiding This.

My thesis was that we will see a dividend cut announced by FSK later in 2025 or early 2026. The reasons are actually quite the same that I had for OCSL's case. For instance, FSK has a significant difference between the in-place portfolio yield and the new loans that it sources from the market. In fact, I would say that FSK has more issues to worry about as, for example, the payment-in-kind component consumes about 15% of the total income, which means that the dividend coverage ratio we see on the surface has to be adjusted to the downside in order to account for these non-cash (PIK) items. Plus, I did not like FSK's late 2023 and early 2024 history on the rising non-accruals, which destroyed a notable portion of NAV.

Now, FSK has reported Q2, 2025 earnings, which are bad.

In my humble opinion, the dividend cut scenario is virtually certain.

The adjusted NII per share declined by $0.05 (on quarter-to-quarter basis), reaching $0.60 per share at the end of Q2. The result was this ugly because of falling spreads and, more importantly, because of increased non-accruals, which increased to 5.3% (from 3.5% in Q1, measured on a cost basis).

As a result of under covered dividend and these additional write-downs, FSK's NAV per share dropped by 6.2%.

Currently, the base dividend coverage is underwater. The ratio stands at ~0.93x, which means that FSK has to either sell assets or take more debt to keep the existing dividend going. Moreover, given that the PIK has now jumped closer to the 20% mark, the actual cash dividend coverage is obviously way lower than what the 0.93x ratio indicates.

Finally, let me conclude this section with the following quote from Michael Craig Forman - Chairman & CEO -, who in the recent earnings call I think indirectly massaged that my assumption of FSK cutting its dividend has turned out to be correct:

In keeping with our long-held view of providing as much transparency as possible to the market, we plan to provide additional details regarding our 2026 dividend strategy on our third quarter earnings call.

The bottom line

The BDC market is not in a good shape.

As I have communicated before, the chances of experiencing positive total returns by just going long the overall BDC factor are definitely lower than 50%, especially if we focus on the 1 - 3 year horizon.

The BDC Q2, 2025 earnings report data points largely confirm that.

In this kind of an environment, we have to be ready to face system-wide dividend cuts.

In the article I have discussed why FSK and OCSL are solid candidates for delivering dividend cuts in the very near term.

data.title: Trulieve: Future Looks Bright, With Some Uncertainty
data.description:
Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (OTCQX:TCNNF) is a cannabis producer and distributor that recently released their Q2 2025 results. I rated the stock a Buy back in May, and while it's up about 30% since then, management's priorities expressed in the earnings call reaffirm my outlook.

I think the stock is still undervalued and doesn't require much growth for the current price to allow upside. Given regulatory risks, however, I think this should be an appropriately sized bet.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I first looked at Trulieve, there were some pressures on my mind that the broader cannabis industry was facing (and continues to face):

  • Excessive debt maturing very soon.
  • Compressed gross margins from too many entrants.

Many lacked sufficient cash balances or the ability to produce free cash flow to repay these upcoming maturities.

Trulieve, unlike some of their peers, held about $328M of cash against about $476M in debt, most of which was not due within the next year. On top of that, their free cash flow had exceeded $100M in 2023 and 2024, indicating sufficient ability to repay and/or refinance.

Additionally, nearly all of their business is done in Florida, which only allows medical use. I believed this would give them a more stable customer base and ability to grow into other state markets as weaker players fall off.

Because of this, I believed the stock was undervalued, even with assumptions of lower baselines of free cash flow and only single-digit growth.

Trulieve Cannabis Q2 2025 Results

The results of Q2 were moderately positive but nothing groundbreaking. Better was the outlook provided by management, but first let's just review some highlights:

  • Revenue for the quarter was $302M, comparable to last year.
  • Gross margin grew from 60% to 61%, YoY.
  • Operating cash flow of $86M and free cash flow of $75M.

For the six months ended June, 2025 seems to be generating less cash from operations than 2024. Some of this was attributable to a larger tax expense than the prior year. As a reminder, cannabis companies are subject to a punitive tax under Section 280E of the tax code. This weakens the cash flow of every cannabis operator.

The other half of the cash flow difference came from fluctuations in working capital.

Looking at the balance sheet, this grew their cash balance to $400M. Long-term debt, meanwhile, remains just above $470M. Also worth noting is the "uncertain tax provision" in the liabilities, which I will address in a moment.

As operations go, there wasn't much change in the footprint, with only two dispensaries added since Q1 (up from 229) and most of the business still heavily concentrated in the Floridian market.

Outlook and Valuation

Probably the most important positives were the priorities that management set for capital allocation during the earnings call. Founder-CEO Kimberly Rivers explained during Q&A:

...we've reiterated again that we will be looking at paying down that debt and we feel like we're in a really strong position to do so and more to come on that as we execute on that throughout the rest of the year.

So debt reduction was the first priority, and it was specified that they intend to retire half of the $365M in private placement notes before the end of the year. She also outlined priorities down the road, after debt is reduced:

...I think that as we're seeing there's additional pressure on other operators, we believe strongly that there will be additional opportunities for potential M&A in the future. I don't think that we're there yet. I think that we're just starting to see some of those processes come to market. We're not interested in taking on loads of other debt from other operators, but we think that asset sales and potentially attractive markets may be coming down the pipe.

This indicates that Rivers agrees with my outlook back in Q1, that Trulieve is properly positioned not only to capture market share but to scoop assets from distressed competition.

Both management and the analysts spoke with optimism on the regulatory and political side. For Florida, there is talk of a modified referendum that would allow adult-cannabis and might have a better chance of succeeding.

As a reminder, the stock tanked in November after the referendum for adult use was defeated, requiring 60% approval to become law and winning only 57%. Until there is another election, there is little to say about it.

On the federal side, the 280E Rescheduling process that began under Biden was put on hold shortly after Trump took office. The Senate recently confirmed his head of the DEA, Will Cole. Cole has said resolving the matter of rescheduling would be a priority. This also brings us to what is likely the primary risk factor for Trulieve.

The company has almost $560M in "uncertain tax liabilities," an amount that continues to grow into 2025. Considering the situation with debt and cash flow, such a large liability could complicate an otherwise healthy balance sheet.

According to their latest Form 10K (pg. 47): the basis of this uncertainty is the following:

The IRS has taken the position Section 280E of the IRC prevents cannabis companies from deducting any business expenses other than those properly included in cost of goods sold...The Company has taken a position that Section 280E of the IRC does not preclude it from deducting ordinary and necessary business expenditures on its tax returns.

Multiple cannabis operators dispute the extent of their tax liability with the IRS under 280E. Chris Roberts of MJBizDaily covered this a year ago, explaining how operators have filed as normal businesses under their legal stance. Some even received tax refunds, to include Trulieve, despite the IRS's firm stance. He also made the following observation:

Among the risks is a 20% penalty the IRS could impose on a company seeking a refund that’s ultimately rejected – that’s 20% on top of the taxes the IRS determines the business owes, plus the cost of paying litigators, he said.

Rivers believes President Trump will follow through on rescheduling, citing his campaign positions and the stances he took in swing states on the issue. Ultimately, to invest in TCNNF, one has to believe that Trump will allow rescheduling and lift this tax burden from cannabis operators. I believe that he will because of his campaign stance, but how exactly does one take a position to manage this risk?

First is entry price. I believe a Discounted Cash Flow model, priced for a 10% discount rate (typical of a market index) shows us that even a low-end estimate of free cash flow and single-digit growth give an intrinsic value of about $1.4B, for $7.37 per share. The current price near $5.50 allows for upside and prices in much of the downside. This is mostly the same as my valuation from last time, only modified with the updated diluted shares outstanding.

Secondly, sizing can help. That is to say, it can be an asymmetric bet in a much larger portfolio. It would not be comparable to a heavy entry into Costco (COST) at a similar discount, as COST doesn't have such a lingering risk factor.

Conclusion

I continue to believe that Trulieve is well positioned and has a real shot at growth as the industry goes through a down cycle. With healthy gross margins, it's not as vulnerable to macro-pressures as many other businesses are. Its main threats are political in nature, and a lack of resolution on Section 280E taxes would spoil the thesis.

Thus, while I will maintain my Buy rating, investors should be mindful of how much they commit to the position and keep up with regulatory developments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
